class_schedule.topic: Mobile Development Landscape and Flutter Fundamentals
learning_objectives: Compare native, hybrid, and cross-platform mobile development approaches
Set up a Flutter development environment
Build and run a first Flutter application
readings: Google Codelabs: Your First Flutter App

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile apps are the primary way billions of people interact with software. This course teaches cross-platform development with Flutter, enabling you to build native-quality iOS and Android apps from a single codebase.
key_concepts: Native vs cross-platform: performance and development speed trade-offs
Flutter architecture: Dart language, widget tree, rendering engine
Widget fundamentals: StatelessWidget vs StatefulWidget
Material Design and Cupertino design systems
Hot reload for rapid iteration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Create a Flutter project, build a simple profile card with an image, name, and bio using Material widgets, and demonstrate hot reload for instant feedback
setup_requirements: Android Studio or VS Code with Flutter extension
Android emulator or iOS simulator configured

review.value: In Flutter, everything is a widget: the app, layouts, text, buttons, even padding. Widgets compose into a tree that Flutter renders to native platform views using its own rendering engine
Flutter injects updated code into the running Dart VM and rebuilds the widget tree, preserving state; changes appear in under a second without restarting the app

learning_objectives: Manage application state using the Provider pattern
Distinguish between ephemeral (local) and application (shared) state
Implement ChangeNotifier for reactive UI updates

lecture_notes.introduction: State management is the hardest problem in mobile development. Provider offers a pragmatic approach that scales from simple apps to complex ones without the boilerplate of more heavyweight solutions.
key_concepts: Ephemeral state: managed locally with setState
Application state: shared across widgets, managed with Provider
ChangeNotifier: notify listeners when state changes
ChangeNotifierProvider: providing state to the widget tree
Consumer and context.watch for reactive rebuilds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a shopping cart app with Provider: product list screen, cart screen, and a shared CartModel that updates both screens when items are added or removed

review.value: When two widgets need the same state, move it to their nearest common ancestor and pass it down; Provider automates this pattern by making state available anywhere in the widget tree
Using Consumer or context.select rebuilds only the widgets that depend on the changed state, not the entire tree; this is critical for performance in large apps

learning_objectives: Implement declarative navigation with Go Router
Build responsive layouts that adapt to different screen sizes
Handle deep linking and route parameters

lecture_notes.introduction: Navigation defines how users move through your app. Responsive layouts ensure your app looks great on phones, tablets, and desktops. Together, they create a polished user experience.
key_concepts: Navigator 2.0 and declarative routing with go_router
Route parameters, query parameters, and path matching
LayoutBuilder and MediaQuery for responsive design
Adaptive layouts: different UI for phone vs tablet
Deep linking: opening specific screens from external URLs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a master-detail layout that shows a list on phones and a side-by-side view on tablets, with deep linking support for sharing specific items
setup_requirements: Multiple device sizes configured in emulator

review.value: Routes are defined as data structures mapping URL paths to widget builders; the framework handles push/pop animations and deep linking automatically, unlike imperative Navigator.push calls
Use LayoutBuilder to check available width: <600dp for phone, 600-900dp for tablet, >900dp for desktop. Build different widget trees for each breakpoint rather than scaling a single layout

learning_objectives: Fetch data from REST APIs using the http package
Persist data locally with SQLite and shared preferences
Handle loading states, errors, and offline scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Most mobile apps are thin clients for backend services. This session covers fetching data from APIs, caching it locally for offline access, and handling the inevitable network failures that mobile environments present.
key_concepts: HTTP client: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE with error handling
JSON serialization and deserialization with model classes
Local storage: shared_preferences for settings, sqflite for structured data
Repository pattern: abstracting data sources behind a common interface
Connectivity awareness: online vs offline behavior
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a news reader that fetches articles from an API, caches them in SQLite for offline reading, and shows a clear loading/error/empty state for each scenario
setup_requirements: Flutter project with http, sqflite, shared_preferences packages

review.value: The repository provides a single interface for data access; it decides whether to fetch from the network, local cache, or both. UI code never knows or cares about the data source
Mobile apps must handle network timeout, server error, no connectivity, and invalid response. Every API call needs loading, success, and error states visible to the user

learning_objectives: Access platform features: camera, location, notifications
Prepare apps for App Store and Play Store submission
Implement platform channels for native code integration

lecture_notes.introduction: The final mile of mobile development is platform integration and publishing. Accessing device hardware, handling permissions, and meeting store review requirements are what turn a Flutter prototype into a production app.
key_concepts: Platform channels: calling native iOS/Android code from Dart
Permissions: requesting camera, location, notification access at runtime
Push notifications: FCM setup and handling
App signing, versioning, and release builds
Store submission: screenshots, descriptions, review guidelines compliance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Add camera capture and location tagging to an existing app using platform plugins, configure Firebase Cloud Messaging for push notifications, and build a release APK
setup_requirements: Flutter project with camera, geolocator, firebase_messaging packages
Physical device for testing hardware features

review.value: Both iOS and Android require requesting sensitive permissions (camera, location) at runtime with a clear explanation of why the app needs them; requesting without explanation results in store rejection

Release builds must be signed with a private key; the corresponding public key is registered with the app store. Losing the signing key means you cannot update the app, so it must be backed up securely

questions.question: Explain the difference between StatelessWidget and StatefulWidget. When should you use each?
questions.answer: StatelessWidget has no mutable state; its build method always produces the same output for the same configuration (props). Use it for static UI elements like labels, icons, and layout containers. StatefulWidget has a companion State object that holds mutable state; calling setState triggers a rebuild with the new state. Use it when the widget needs to change in response to user interaction or asynchronous events (e.g., a checkbox, a form field, an animation).

rubric.key: Correct definitions of both widget types

questions.question: Write a ChangeNotifier class called CounterModel with an increment method and a count getter. Then show how to provide it with ChangeNotifierProvider and consume it with Consumer.
Explain the repository pattern in the context of a mobile app that fetches data from an API and caches it locally. Why is this pattern valuable?
questions.answer: The repository pattern creates an abstraction layer between the data sources (remote API, local database) and the rest of the application. The repository interface exposes methods like getArticles() without revealing whether data comes from the network or local cache. The implementation decides: check the cache first, fall back to the API if the cache is stale or empty, and update the cache after a successful API call. This is valuable because: (1) UI code does not need to change when you switch data sources. (2) Testing is easier with mock repositories. (3) Offline support is implemented in one place. (4) Caching strategy can be changed without affecting the rest of the app.

questions.question: A Flutter app crashes when the user navigates back from a screen that makes an API call. The error says 'setState called after dispose'. Explain the cause and the fix.
questions.answer: The API call is asynchronous. If the user navigates away before the response arrives, the widget is disposed (removed from the tree). When the callback runs and calls setState, the State object is no longer mounted, causing the error. Fix: Check the mounted property before calling setState. Better: cancel the async operation in the dispose method using a CancelToken (for HTTP) or by setting a flag. Best: use a state management solution like Provider where the model outlives the widget and the widget rebuilds reactively without needing setState.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies async callback after dispose
Mentions canceling the async operation in dispose
Suggests state management as a structural fix

major_assignments.title: Full-Featured Mobile Application
major_assignments.description: Design and build a complete cross-platform mobile application with Flutter that includes multiple screens, state management, API integration, local storage, and platform feature integration. The app should solve a real user need and be polished enough for store submission.
objectives: Build a multi-screen Flutter app with proper navigation and state management
Integrate with a REST API for data and implement local caching
Access at least one platform feature (camera, location, or notifications)
Produce a polished app ready for store submission
deliverables: Release builds for Android (APK) and iOS (IPA or simulator build)
App store listing assets: icon, screenshots, description
Design document (2 pages) explaining architecture and technology choices

criteria: All specified features work correctly on both platforms
Navigation handles edge cases (back button, deep links)
API integration handles loading, error, and empty states
Local storage provides meaningful offline capability
State management uses Provider with proper separation
Repository pattern abstracts data sources
Code is organized by feature with clear module boundaries
No unnecessary rebuilds or performance issues
UI follows Material Design guidelines consistently
App is responsive across phone and tablet sizes
Loading and error states provide clear feedback
Animations and transitions feel native and polished
Architecture document explains key decisions
README includes setup and build instructions
Code has meaningful comments where needed
Store listing assets are professional quality
Complete cross-platform app with robust feature set
Clean architecture with Provider and repository pattern
Polished UI with proper error handling and offline support
Professional documentation and store-ready assets

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of Flutter and mobile concepts

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam on Flutter fundamentals

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all mobile development topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion is encouraged. All code must be written individually unless pair programming is assigned.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Using templates or AI-generated code without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Flutter SDK, Android Studio or VS Code, and either an Android device/emulator or iOS simulator. Lab computers are available.
